tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying anaphylaxis due to shellfish exposure ensures time-critical management and appropriate patient counseling.
Providing immediate and take-home epinephrine (autoinjector) is the cornerstone of anaphylaxis treatment and reduces mortality risk from recurrence.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly communicates the working diagnosis as anaphylaxis and links it to shellfish exposure (must include the terms "anaphylaxis" and "shellfish").
Agent explains that epinephrine is first-line treatment for anaphylaxis and provides education on an epinephrine autoinjector (must include the terms "epinephrine" and "autoinjector").
Before prescribing, agent performs an allergy check (critical): asks about medication allergies and prior reactions, and addresses any reported allergy in the plan.

Before prescribing, agent performs a contraindication check (critical): screens for relevant contraindications/precautions and states that there are no absolute contraindications to epinephrine in anaphylaxis, while noting key precautions if present.

Before prescribing, agent performs a drug interaction check (noncritical): reviews current medications (e.g., beta-blockers, MAOIs, tricyclics) and acknowledges potential interaction/attenuated response without delaying epinephrine.

Agent recommends emergency management appropriate to suspected/confirmed anaphylaxis (e.g., call EMS/ED evaluation, observation for biphasic reaction) and does not suggest antihistamines or steroids as substitutes for epinephrine.
Agent provides safety-net instructions: recurrence/biphasic symptoms, when to use the autoinjector, and to seek emergency care after use.
Agent uses clear, non-alarming language, checks understanding (teach-back), and ensures the patient knows to avoid shellfish and potential cross-contact until evaluated.
